concepts.name: Carbohydrates – Definition and General Formula
concepts.explanation: Carbohydrates are optically active polyhydroxy aldehydes or ketones, or compounds that yield such units on hydrolysis. They have the general formula Cₓ(H₂O)ᵧ, but not all compounds fitting this formula are carbohydrates (e.g., acetic acid). Examples: glucose, fructose, starch, cellulose.

concepts.name: Classification of Carbohydrates – Monosaccharides, Oligosaccharides, Polysaccharides
concepts.explanation: Monosaccharides cannot be hydrolysed further (e.g., glucose, fructose). Oligosaccharides yield 2-10 monosaccharide units on hydrolysis (e.g., sucrose, maltose, lactose). Polysaccharides yield many monosaccharide units (e.g., starch, cellulose, glycogen).

concepts.name: Reducing and Non-Reducing Sugars
concepts.explanation: Reducing sugars reduce Fehling's and Tollens' reagents (all monosaccharides are reducing). Non-reducing sugars do not reduce these reagents (e.g., sucrose, where the reducing groups are involved in glycosidic linkage).

concepts.name: Monosaccharides – Aldoses and Ketoses
concepts.explanation: Monosaccharides are classified by the functional group (aldose – aldehyde; ketose – ketone) and the number of carbon atoms (triose, tetrose, pentose, hexose). Example: glucose is an aldohexose; fructose is a ketohexose.

concepts.name: Glucose – Preparation
concepts.explanation: Glucose is prepared by: (i) hydrolysis of sucrose with dilute HCl or H₂SO₄ (gives glucose + fructose); (ii) hydrolysis of starch with dilute H₂SO₄ at 393 K under pressure.

concepts.name: Glucose – Structure Evidences
concepts.explanation: Glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) is an aldohexose. Evidence: (i) molecular formula C₆H₁₂O₆; (ii) with HI gives n-hexane (straight chain); (iii) with NH₂OH gives oxime and with HCN gives cyanohydrin (carbonyl group); (iv) with Br₂ water gives gluconic acid (aldehyde group); (v) acetylation gives glucose pentaacetate (five -OH groups); (vi) with HNO₃ gives saccharic acid (primary alcohol).

concepts.name: Glucose – Configuration (D and L)
concepts.explanation: D and L notation refers to the configuration relative to glyceraldehyde. D-(+)-glucose has the -OH on the lowest asymmetric carbon on the right side. 'D' is not related to dextrorotatory; the (+) sign indicates optical rotation.

concepts.name: Glucose – Cyclic Structure (Pyranose Ring)
concepts.explanation: Glucose exists as a cyclic hemiacetal (six-membered pyranose ring) formed by the addition of the -OH at C5 to the aldehyde group. This explains: (i) absence of Schiff's test; (ii) no NaHSO₃ addition; (iii) existence of α and β forms (anomers).

concepts.name: Anomers – α and β Forms of Glucose
concepts.explanation: α-D-glucose and β-D-glucose are anomers, differing only in the configuration at C1 (anomeric carbon). α-form (m.p. 419 K) is obtained from cold concentrated solution; β-form (m.p. 423 K) from hot saturated solution.

concepts.name: Fructose – Structure
concepts.explanation: Fructose is a ketohexose (C₆H₁₂O₆). It has a ketone group at C2, a straight chain of six carbons, and belongs to the D-series. It is laevorotatory D-(-)-fructose. It exists as a five-membered furanose ring.

concepts.name: Disaccharides – Glycosidic Linkage
concepts.explanation: Disaccharides are formed by two monosaccharide units joined by a glycosidic linkage (an oxide linkage formed by loss of water between the anomeric carbon of one sugar and a hydroxyl group of the other).

concepts.name: Sucrose – Structure and Properties
concepts.explanation: Sucrose (C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁) on hydrolysis gives equimolar glucose and fructose. It is a non-reducing sugar (glycosidic linkage between C1 of α-D-glucose and C2 of β-D-fructose). Hydrolysis gives invert sugar (dextrorotation to laevorotation).

concepts.name: Maltose – Structure and Properties
concepts.explanation: Maltose is composed of two α-D-glucose units linked by C1-C4 glycosidic linkage. It is a reducing sugar (free aldehyde at C1 of the second glucose unit).

concepts.name: Lactose – Structure and Properties
concepts.explanation: Lactose (milk sugar) is composed of β-D-galactose and β-D-glucose linked by C1-C4 glycosidic linkage. It is a reducing sugar.

concepts.name: Polysaccharides – Starch (Amylose and Amylopectin)
concepts.explanation: Starch is a polymer of α-D-glucose. It has two components: amylose (15-20%, water-soluble, unbranched, 200-1000 units, C1-C4 linkage) and amylopectin (80-85%, water-insoluble, branched, C1-C4 chain with C1-C6 branching).

concepts.name: Polysaccharides – Cellulose
concepts.explanation: Cellulose is a straight-chain polysaccharide of β-D-glucose units linked by C1-C4 glycosidic linkages. It is the most abundant organic substance in plants and a major component of cell walls.

concepts.name: Polysaccharides – Glycogen
concepts.explanation: Glycogen is the animal storage polysaccharide (animal starch). It is similar to amylopectin but more highly branched. It is found in liver, muscles, and brain.

concepts.name: Importance of Carbohydrates
concepts.explanation: Carbohydrates are essential for life: (i) energy source (honey, starch, glycogen); (ii) structural materials (cellulose in plants); (iii) raw materials for textiles, paper, and breweries; (iv) components of nucleic acids (ribose and deoxyribose).

concepts.name: Amino Acids – Structure and Classification
concepts.explanation: Amino acids contain -NH₂ and -COOH groups. α-Amino acids have the amino group on the carbon adjacent to the carboxyl group. They are classified as acidic (more -COOH), basic (more -NH₂), or neutral (equal numbers).

concepts.name: Essential and Non-Essential Amino Acids
concepts.explanation: Essential amino acids cannot be synthesised in the body and must be obtained from the diet (e.g., valine, leucine, isoleucine, phenylalanine, tryptophan, methionine, lysine, threonine, histidine, arginine). Non-essential amino acids can be synthesised in the body.

concepts.name: Zwitter Ion and Amphoteric Behaviour of Amino Acids
concepts.explanation: Amino acids exist as dipolar ions (zwitter ions) in aqueous solution, with both positive and negative charges. They show amphoteric behaviour, reacting with both acids and bases. This explains their high melting points and water solubility.

concepts.name: Optical Activity of Amino Acids
concepts.explanation: Except glycine, all α-amino acids are optically active because the α-carbon is asymmetric (chiral). Naturally occurring amino acids have the L-configuration.

concepts.name: Peptide Bond and Peptide Linkage
concepts.explanation: A peptide bond is an amide bond formed between the -COOH group of one amino acid and the -NH₂ group of another, with the elimination of water. The -CO-NH- linkage is called a peptide linkage.

concepts.name: Polypeptides and Proteins
concepts.explanation: A dipeptide has two amino acids; tripeptide has three; polypeptides have more than ten amino acids. Proteins are polypeptides with more than 100 amino acids and molecular mass > 10,000 u (e.g., insulin has 51 amino acids).

concepts.name: Fibrous and Globular Proteins
concepts.explanation: Fibrous proteins have polypeptide chains arranged parallel, held by H-bonds and disulphide bonds; insoluble in water (e.g., keratin, myosin). Globular proteins have coiled polypeptide chains with a spherical shape; soluble in water (e.g., insulin, albumins).

concepts.name: Primary Structure of Proteins
concepts.explanation: The primary structure is the specific sequence of amino acids in the polypeptide chain. Any change in this sequence creates a different protein.

concepts.name: Secondary Structure of Proteins – α-Helix and β-Pleated Sheet
concepts.explanation: Secondary structure refers to the regular folding of the polypeptide chain due to H-bonding between -CO- and -NH- groups. α-Helix: right-handed screw with H-bonds between adjacent turns. β-Pleated sheet: stretched chains held side by side by intermolecular H-bonds.

concepts.name: Tertiary and Quaternary Structures of Proteins
concepts.explanation: Tertiary structure: overall folding of the polypeptide chain, stabilised by H-bonds, disulphide linkages, van der Waals forces, and electrostatic interactions. Quaternary structure: spatial arrangement of multiple polypeptide chains (sub-units).

concepts.name: Denaturation of Proteins
concepts.explanation: Denaturation is the loss of the native three-dimensional structure and biological activity of a protein due to physical (temperature) or chemical (pH change) factors. The primary structure remains intact. Example: boiling an egg (albumin denatures).

concepts.name: Enzymes – Biocatalysts
concepts.explanation: Enzymes are biocatalysts (mostly globular proteins) that speed up biochemical reactions under mild conditions. They are highly specific for substrates and reactions. Named after the substrate (e.g., maltase) or reaction (e.g., oxidoreductase).

concepts.name: Mechanism of Enzyme Action
concepts.explanation: Enzymes reduce the activation energy of reactions. For example, the activation energy for sucrose hydrolysis is 6.22 kJ/mol with acid but only 2.15 kJ/mol with the enzyme sucrase. The enzyme-substrate complex forms, lowering the energy barrier.

concepts.name: Vitamins – Definition and Classification
concepts.explanation: Vitamins are organic compounds required in small amounts in the diet for normal growth and health. They are classified as fat-soluble (A, D, E, K – stored in liver and adipose tissue) and water-soluble (B-complex and C – excreted in urine, not stored).

concepts.name: Fat-Soluble Vitamins – A, D, E, K
concepts.explanation: Vitamin A (retinol): sources – fish liver oil, carrots; deficiency – xerophthalmia, night blindness. Vitamin D (calciferol): sources – sunlight, fish, egg yolk; deficiency – rickets (children), osteomalacia (adults). Vitamin E (tocopherol): sources – vegetable oils; deficiency – RBC fragility. Vitamin K (phylloquinone): sources – green leafy vegetables; deficiency – increased clotting time.

concepts.name: Water-Soluble Vitamins – B-Complex and C
concepts.explanation: Vitamin B₁ (thiamine): sources – yeast, milk; deficiency – beri-beri. Vitamin B₂ (riboflavin): sources – milk, egg white; deficiency – cheilosis. Vitamin B₆ (pyridoxine): sources – yeast, egg yolk; deficiency – convulsions. Vitamin B₁₂ (cyanocobalamin): sources – meat, fish, eggs; deficiency – pernicious anaemia. Vitamin C (ascorbic acid): sources – citrus fruits, amla; deficiency – scurvy.

concepts.name: Nucleic Acids – DNA and RNA
concepts.explanation: Nucleic acids are polymers of nucleotides. DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) contains the sugar 2-deoxyribose and the bases adenine (A), guanine (G), cytosine (C), and thymine (T). RNA (ribonucleic acid) contains ribose and the bases A, G, C, and uracil (U) instead of thymine.

concepts.name: Nucleosides and Nucleotides
concepts.explanation: A nucleoside is formed by the attachment of a base to the 1' position of a sugar. A nucleotide is a nucleoside linked to phosphoric acid at the 5' position of the sugar. Nucleotides are the building blocks of nucleic acids.

concepts.name: DNA – Double Helix Structure
concepts.explanation: DNA has a double-stranded helical structure (Watson-Crick model). The two strands are complementary and held together by hydrogen bonds between specific base pairs: A=T (two H-bonds) and G≡C (three H-bonds). The strands are antiparallel.

concepts.name: RNA – Structure and Types
concepts.explanation: RNA is single-stranded and can fold back on itself. The three types are: messenger RNA (mRNA – carries genetic information from DNA to ribosomes), ribosomal RNA (rRNA – structural component of ribosomes), and transfer RNA (tRNA – carries amino acids to ribosomes).

concepts.name: Functions of Nucleic Acids
concepts.explanation: DNA is the chemical basis of heredity (stores genetic information) and can self-duplicate during cell division. Nucleic acids are involved in protein synthesis: the message for protein synthesis is in DNA, and RNA molecules carry out the synthesis.

concepts.name: Hormones – Chemical Nature and Functions
concepts.explanation: Hormones are intercellular messengers produced by endocrine glands. Chemically, they can be steroids (estrogens, androgens), polypeptides (insulin), or amino acid derivatives (epinephrine). Functions: regulate blood glucose (insulin/glucagon), mediate stress responses (epinephrine), control growth and development (growth hormones, sex hormones), and regulate metabolism (thyroxine).
